records.ety: [F. juste, L. justus, fr. jus right, law, justice; orig., that which is fitting; akin to Skr. yu to join. Cf. Injury, Judge, Jury, Giusto.]

defs: Conforming or conformable to rectitude or justice; not doing wrong to any; violating no right or obligation; upright; righteous; honest; true; -- said both of persons and things.
quotes: There is not a just man upon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not.
Just balances, just weights, . . . shall ye have.
How should man be just with God?
We know your grace to be a man. Just and upright.

defs: Not transgressing the requirement of truth and propriety; conformed to the truth of things, to reason, or to a proper standard; exact; normal; reasonable; regular; due; as, a just statement; a just inference.
quotes: Just of thy word, in every thought sincere.
The prince is here at hand: pleaseth your lordship To meet his grace just distance 'tween our armies.
He was a comely personage, a little above just stature.
Fire fitted with just materials casts a constant heat.
When all The war shall stand ranged in its just array.
Their names alone would make a just volume.

defs: Rendering or disposed to render to each one his due; equitable; fair; impartial; as, just judge.
quotes: Men are commonly so just to virtue and goodness as to praise it in others, even when they do not practice it themselves.

defs: Precisely; exactly; -- in place, time, or degree; neither more nor less than is stated.
quotes: And having just enough, not covet more.
The god Pan guided my hand just to the heart of the beast.
To-night, at Herne's oak, just 'twixt twelve and one.
Just at the point of death.

defs: Barely; merely; scarcely; only; by a very small space or time; as, he just missed the train; just too late.
quotes: A soft Etesian gale But just inspired and gently swelled the sail.
